title: Contains Duplicate (seen-set)
code: if (!seen.Add(x)) // Add returns false if already present

frames.caption: Walk the array left to right. For each x, ask the set "have I seen you?" before adding it — the first time Add reports x is already present, the array has a duplicate.
frames.note: The brute force compares every pair in O(n^2). The hashing insight: instead of re-scanning, remember what you have already seen in a HashSet, so each "seen it?" question is O(1) average. Trading O(n) extra memory for O(1) membership collapses the whole check into a single O(n) pass with an early exit on the first repeat.

frames.caption: x = 1: ask seen.Add(1) — does the set already contain 1?
frames.note: The single call seen.Add(x) asks and answers "is x already here?" in one O(1) average operation. Looking at the set before committing is what makes the check correct: an element is compared only against values seen strictly earlier, never against itself.

frames.caption: x = 1: seen.Add(1) returns true — 1 was new, so it is inserted. No duplicate yet; advance.
frames.note: HashSet.Add doubles as the membership test: a true return means x was not present, so it was just inserted and we keep scanning. This "ask, then add" fusion is why no separate Contains call is needed — one O(1) call both checks and records. The set now holds every value seen so far, which is exactly the question we will ask of the next element.

frames.caption: x = 2: ask seen.Add(2) — does the set already contain 2?
frames.note: The single call seen.Add(x) asks and answers "is x already here?" in one O(1) average operation. Looking at the set before committing is what makes the check correct: an element is compared only against values seen strictly earlier, never against itself.

frames.caption: x = 2: seen.Add(2) returns true — 2 was new, so it is inserted. No duplicate yet; advance.
frames.note: HashSet.Add doubles as the membership test: a true return means x was not present, so it was just inserted and we keep scanning. This "ask, then add" fusion is why no separate Contains call is needed — one O(1) call both checks and records. The set now holds every value seen so far, which is exactly the question we will ask of the next element.

frames.caption: x = 3: ask seen.Add(3) — does the set already contain 3?
frames.note: The single call seen.Add(x) asks and answers "is x already here?" in one O(1) average operation. Looking at the set before committing is what makes the check correct: an element is compared only against values seen strictly earlier, never against itself.

frames.caption: x = 3: seen.Add(3) returns true — 3 was new, so it is inserted. No duplicate yet; advance.
frames.note: HashSet.Add doubles as the membership test: a true return means x was not present, so it was just inserted and we keep scanning. This "ask, then add" fusion is why no separate Contains call is needed — one O(1) call both checks and records. The set now holds every value seen so far, which is exactly the question we will ask of the next element.

frames.caption: x = 1: ask seen.Add(1) — does the set already contain 1?
frames.note: The single call seen.Add(x) asks and answers "is x already here?" in one O(1) average operation. Looking at the set before committing is what makes the check correct: an element is compared only against values seen strictly earlier, never against itself.

frames.caption: x = 1: seen.Add(1) returns false — 1 is already in the set. That is a duplicate, so return true immediately.
frames.note: A false return from Add is the duplicate signal: the value was inserted on an earlier iteration, so this is its second appearance. We return on the spot rather than finishing the scan — the question was only "is there any duplicate?", and one is enough. The sort-then-scan alternative is O(n log n) time but O(1) extra space; this set-based pass is O(n) time, O(n) space.
